title: Portfolio Management
description: Construction of optimal portfolios using Markowitz theory (Mean-Variance) and Sharpe ratio maximization. Efficient frontier analysis and asset allocation.
longDescription: This project implements the Markowitz Mean-Variance optimization framework to construct efficient investment portfolios. It calculates the expected returns, covariance matrix, and the efficient frontier. The model allows for the identification of the Global Minimum Variance (GMV) portfolio and the Tangency Portfolio (Maximum Sharpe Ratio). Key features include visualization of the risk-return tradeoff and dynamic asset allocation strategies based on historical data.

title: Systemic Risk & CoVaR Modeling
description: An analysis of how four major US companies affect the overall risk of the stock market during extreme financial conditions.
longDescription: This analysis measures the ``domino effect'' that four major companies (JPMorgan, Progressive, NVIDIA, and NextEra Energy) could have on the S&P 500 during a market crash. Using statistical models, the project evaluates how risk spreads across sectors in extreme scenarios. The results revealed an interesting insight: despite NVIDIA having the most volatile stock, its risk is mostly isolated. In contrast, the utility company (NextEra Energy) actually poses the highest contagion risk to the wider market, an effect that was clearly amplified during the COVID-19 crisis.

title: Exotic Options Pricing via Monte Carlo Simulation
description: A computational finance project using Monte Carlo simulations to calculate the fair value of complex derivatives, such as Basket and Barrier options.
longDescription: This project uses Monte Carlo simulations to estimate the price of complex financial products. It focuses on two main types: Basket Options, whose value depends on a group of correlated assets, and Barrier Options, which only pay out if the asset's price stays within certain limits over time. Beyond just calculating prices, the project analyzes the code's efficiency, finding the right balance between the number of simulated scenarios and computational speed to minimize errors and get accurate results.

title: Macroeconomic VAR Analysis: Evaluating Monetary Policy Shocks
description: An econometric analysis project using Vector Autoregression (VAR) and Impulse Response Functions (IRFs) to measure the impact of Federal Reserve interest rate changes on key economic indicators.
longDescription: This project models the dynamic relationships between major macroeconomic variables—specifically inflation, unemployment, federal interest rates, and 10-year treasury bonds—using a Vector Autoregression (VAR) framework. After determining the optimal lag structure using the Akaike Information Criterion (AIC), it applies structural identification techniques like Cholesky decomposition and Monte Carlo-based sign restrictions to isolate monetary policy shocks. Ultimately, the project generates Impulse Response Function (IRF) plots to visualize how sudden shifts in interest rates ripple through the broader economy over a 24-month horizon.

title: Interest Rate Derivatives Pricing & Volatility Modeling
description: Calculating the fair value of complex interest rate products, such as Swaps and Caps, using real-world financial models.
longDescription: This analysis focuses on determining the accurate price of long-term fixed-income products, specifically a 20-year Interest Rate Swap and an Interest Rate Cap linked to the Euribor. Rather than relying on purely theoretical math, the valuation applies real banking conventions, trading calendars, and market volatility models. This ensures the pricing reflects actual market conditions and provides a realistic view of the risks and costs associated with long-term interest rate fluctuations.
